 {"id":"ft-2oph2.2","title":"[ft-2oph2][perf] Reuse chunked scan buffer for definitive trigger scan","description":"# Goal
Eliminate duplicate full-buffer accumulation in the chunked scan pipeline when both trigger scanning and compression are enabled.

## Scope
- File: `crates/frankenterm-core/src/scan_pipeline.rs`
- Reuse the existing uncompressed chunk buffer as the definitive flush-time trigger input whenever compression is enabled, instead of also appending the same bytes into `trigger_data_buffer`.
- Preserve exact batch/chunk trigger parity and compression behavior.
- Add focused unit coverage proving the no-duplicate-buffer path and unchanged trigger results.

## Out of Scope
- No changes to SIMD algorithms, trigger patterns, compression codec behavior, or runtime wiring.
- No changes to unrelated runtime/event/storage files.

## Validation
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --lib scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo test -p frankenterm-core --test proptest_scan_pipeline -- --nocapture`
- `rch exec -- cargo bench -p frankenterm-core --bench scan_pipeline --no-run`

## Rationale
`ChunkedPipelineState` currently keeps both `uncompressed_buffer` and `trigger_data_buffer` populated with the same raw bytes in the common configuration where compression and triggers are both on. This inflates memory traffic and buffer growth on the hot path without changing behavior.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TealLotus","created_at":"2026-03-31T18:02:43.603376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917970Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T17:20:17.917717Z","close_reason":"Implemented chunked scan buffer reuse, strengthened compression-enabled trigger parity tests, and validated via rch-backed scan_pipeline/proptest/bench coverage; remaining repo-wide failures were unrelated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-2xnop","title":"[MEDIUM] pty CommandBuilder serde proptests skip serialized unix umask state","description":"[MEDIUM] Recent commit c664dc39 (test: extend pty serde proptests) added frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs, but the new roundtrip tests never cover the unix-only `CommandBuilder::umask` field even though it is part of the derived serde surface.

Evidence:
- frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs:202-210 derives Serialize/Deserialize for CommandBuilder and includes `#[cfg(unix)] pub(crate) umask: Option<libc::mode_t>`.
- frankenterm/pty/src/cmdbuilder.rs:423-425 exposes `CommandBuilder::umask(...)` to mutate that serialized field.
- frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs:35-52 `build_command(...)` never sets umask.
- frankenterm/pty/tests/proptest_cmdbuilder_serde.rs:57-123 only asserts argv/cwd/env/controlling_tty, so a serde regression that drops or corrupts `umask` would still pass.

Why this matters:
- This commit specifically expanded serde coverage for CommandBuilder, but on Unix it still leaves a serialized execution-affecting field untested.
- `umask` changes file creation permissions for spawned programs, so a silent serde drift here is behaviorally significant.

Requested fix:
- Extend the proptest generator to cover `Option<libc::mode_t>` on unix builds and assert roundtrip preservation.
- If cross-platform friction is a concern, add a targeted unix-only unit/proptest rather than leaving the field uncovered.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-20T05:52:51.639844Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T06:08:27.030677Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T06:08:27.029943Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-326s9","title":"Fix bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls test — config shape contradicts production logic","description":"# Pre-existing mTLS test failure

## Background
Discovered during ft-xbnl0.2.4 tick 359 verification work. The test
`distributed::tests::bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls` at
`crates/frankenterm-core/src/distributed.rs:3805` fails reproducibly with:

    client bundle: MissingClientCaPath

## Reproduction
```
cargo test -p frankenterm-core --features distributed \
    --lib bundle_acceptor_connector_mtls -- --nocapture
```

## Root cause
The test constructs a `client_cfg` with `auth_mode =
DistributedAuthMode::Mtls` but without `client_ca_path` populated:

```rust
let mut client_cfg = DistributedConfig::default();
client_cfg.enabled = true;
client_cfg.auth_mode = DistributedAuthMode::Mtls;
client_cfg.tls.enabled = true;
client_cfg.tls.cert_path = Some(client_cert.path().display().to_string());
client_cfg.tls.key_path = Some(client_key.path().display().to_string());
// client_ca_path intentionally left as None.
```

Then it calls `build_tls_bundle(&client_cfg, Some(ca_cert.path()))`. Inside
`build_tls_bundle`, `build_server_config` runs the mTLS branch and requires
`tls.client_ca_path` (the cert used to verify INCOMING client certs). It is
None → `DistributedTlsError::MissingClientCaPath`.

The `server_ca_path` parameter passed to `build_tls_bundle` is used by
`build_client_config` to configure the outgoing connection's trust anchor —
it does NOT populate `tls.client_ca_path`.

## The tension
- `ft-xbnl0.2.4` tick 337 test `build_tls_bundle_rejects_mtls_without_client_ca_path`
  explicitly pins "Mtls auth without client_ca_path → `MissingClientCaPath`"
  as the intended production contract.
- The failing test expects the opposite (that this config shape succeeds).

## Options
1. Fix the test: set `client_cfg.tls.client_ca_path = Some(ca_cert.path().display().to_string())` alongside the existing cert/key.
2. Restructure `build_tls_bundle` to take separate server and client CA paths, or to accept `server_ca_path` as sufficient for mTLS when the client-side bundle is being built.
3. Deprecate the failing test if the config shape was only ever tentative.

## Attribution
- Authored: jemanuel on 2026-04-10 (commit 147b22176).
- Never fixed or touched since.
- Observed failing on 2026-04-18 during ft-xbnl0.2.4 verification sweep.

## Not a blocker for ft-xbnl0.2.4
The bead-scoped check script (`scripts/check_ft_xbnl0_2_4.sh`) uses `--lib tls_`
filter which does not catch this test (the name has `mtls` with no `tls_`
substring). All 73 tests in that filter pass. The failure surfaces only on
broader `--lib distributed::tests::` filter.

See: `docs/ft-xbnl0-2-4-completion-evidence.md §6.1.2`.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"jemanuel","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:04:36.191788Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-18T18:11:31.047438Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T18:11:31.047196Z","close_reason":"Fixed by tick 362 (commit c799aad4): populated client_cfg.tls.client_ca_path in the test, matching the tick-337 positive-guard contract.  {"id":"ft-9d4a3","title":"[MEDIUM] EventBus stats overreport queued backlog after subscribers drain channel","description":"Review finding in crates/frankenterm-core/src/events.rs:655-674 and 1942-1970. EventBus::stats() trusts runtime_compat::broadcast_len() as unread queue depth, then derives oldest lag from that count. Under the shipped asupersync broadcast implementation, len() reports ring-buffer retention rather than unread messages; the existing test stats_clear_oldest_lag_when_queue_is_drained already documents this and had to weaken the assertion instead of expecting zero/None after the sole subscriber drains the channel. Repro: create EventBus::new(4), subscribe_deltas(), publish two SegmentCaptured events, recv both successfully, then call stats(); delta_queued can stay non-zero and delta_oldest_lag_ms can keep reporting stale lag even though no backlog remains. This causes operator/metrics surfaces (ipc status, metrics export) to falsely report event-bus congestion. Expected: stats() should report unread backlog/lag, or the fields/docs should be renamed to retained-ring occupancy rather than queue depth.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-24T18:14:13.498770Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-24T19:16:04.232903Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T19:16:04.232646Z","close_reason":"Track unread backlog from slowest subscriber instead of retained ring occupancy","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["patterns","review"]}

 {"id":"ft-9xbxe","title":"mcp_tools test helper hits asupersync vs fastmcp_rust::asupersync version conflict","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4812\` cannot compile under
\`--features mcp\`:

\`\`\`
error[E0308]: mismatched types
    --> crates/frankenterm-core/src/mcp_tools.rs:4812:25
     |
4812 |         McpContext::new(asupersync::Cx::for_testing(), 1)
     |         --------------- ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
     |                         expected \`fastmcp_rust::Cx\`,
     |                         found \`asupersync::Cx\`
     |
note: there are multiple different versions of crate \`asupersync\` in
      the dependency graph
\`\`\`

\`McpContext::new\` (defined in \`fastmcp_rust\`) expects
\`fastmcp_rust::Cx\` (i.e. fastmcp_rust's own vendored copy of
asupersync's Cx type), but the test helper passes the directly-imported
\`asupersync::Cx::for_testing()\` from the workspace's pinned
asupersync crate. With version drift between fastmcp_rust's vendored
asupersync (0.2.6 per the older fastmcp_rust pin) and the workspace
asupersync (0.2.9 per /Users/jemanuel/.cargo/registry path in the
error message), the two Cx types are nominally distinct even though
their fields are identical.

## Concrete failure path

1. Run \`cargo test --no-run --lib -p frankenterm-core --features mcp\`.
2. Compiler tries to build the \`#[cfg(feature = "mcp")]\` test module
   in mcp_tools.rs.
3. \`test_mcp_context()\` at line 4811-4813 fails to type-check because
   \`McpContext::new\` from fastmcp_rust expects \`fastmcp_rust::Cx\` but
   the call site passes \`asupersync::Cx\` from the workspace's
   asupersync.
4. **Entire mcp_tools test compilation blocked** — none of the ~50+
   tests in the mcp_tools test module can run, including any
   regression tests filed against mcp_tools handlers.

## Reproduction

\`\`\`
CC=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang \\
CXX=/opt/homebrew/opt/llvm/bin/clang++ \\
CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/ft-rv-p0-target \\
cargo test --no-run --lib -p frankenterm-core \\
  --no-default-features --features mcp 2>&1 | grep -B2 -A10 E0308
\`\`\`

## Severity rationale

- Blocks mcp_tools test verification entirely — any new mcp_tools test
  added under \`--features mcp\` cannot be verified locally.
- Real defect-finding work in the mcp surface (e.g. ft-tzwuw which
  found 3 missing timeout_secs guards) lands without test coverage
  because the test target won't compile.
- Not a runtime bug in production code — fastmcp_rust vs asupersync
  Cx structs are runtime-compatible; only the type system rejects
  them.

## Fix sketch

One of:
1. Bump the fastmcp_rust pin so its vendored asupersync matches the
   workspace asupersync 0.2.9 (preferred — single source of truth).
2. Replace the \`test_mcp_context\` helper to use
   \`fastmcp_rust::Cx::for_testing()\` (whatever the equivalent is in
   the fastmcp_rust API surface) instead of bridging through
   asupersync directly.
3. Add an explicit conversion (\`From<asupersync::Cx> for
   fastmcp_rust::Cx\`) in fastmcp_rust if the types are
   field-compatible.

(1) is the cleanest because it eliminates the duplicated dependency.
(2) is the smallest local change.

## Scope

Diagnostic only — fix likely lives in fastmcp_rust pin (Cargo.toml)
or the test helper. Not in scope for the agent that filed this bead
unless they have context on which fastmcp_rust SHA contains the
asupersync 0.2.9 alignment.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:52:59.938463Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:26.989722Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:12:26.989533Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-a0up5","title":"ingest::trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes returns more than max_bytes when last char straddles the cap","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/ingest.rs:1405\` — \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes(text, max_bytes)\`
silently returns a string LARGER than \`max_bytes\` when the last
character would not fit. The function name and downstream callers
expect a hard \`max_bytes\` cap.

## Concrete failure path

Trace \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("中", 1)\` (中 is 3 UTF-8 bytes):

1. text.len()=3, max_bytes=1. \`text.len() <= max_bytes\` (3 <= 1) is
   false; max_bytes != 0; continue.
2. start = 3.saturating_sub(1) = 2. Position 2 is in the middle of 中.
3. Snap-forward loop: is_char_boundary(2)=false → start=3.
   is_char_boundary(3)=true (text.len() is always a boundary). But the
   loop guard is \`start < text.len()\`, which is false at start=3.
   Exit with start=3.
4. \`start >= text.len()\` (3 >= 3) && max_bytes > 0 → enter the
   "fall back to last full character" branch (line 1419-1430).
5. The fallback walks backward from text.len()=3 to find the start of
   the last character (position 0 for 中), then \`saturating_sub(1)\`
   off-by-ones to position 0. (The off-by-one here is a separate issue
   from the contract violation but produces the same bytes.)
6. Returns \`text[0..]\` = "中" — **3 bytes when max_bytes was 1**.

Symmetric case: \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("a中", 2)\` returns "中"
(3 bytes when 2 was requested).

## Caller impact

\`enforce_segment_size_for_persistence\` (line 1434) wraps this and
captures \`kept_bytes = truncated_content.len()\` in
\`SegmentSizeEnforcement\`. If \`kept_bytes > max_bytes\`, the
\`SegmentSizeEnforcement.kept_bytes\` field reports the violation
correctly — but the actual segment is still oversized, and:

* The truncation gap reason format string says \`"max_bytes={max}"\` —
  misleading because the kept bytes exceed it.
* Downstream consumers that allocate \`max_segment_bytes\`-sized
  buffers, set FTS5 column truncation, or enforce DB-level CHECK
  constraints on segment length get unexpectedly large input.
* Any future caller that does \`assert!(result.len() <= max_bytes)\` as
  a defensive check would fire on a config that's perfectly valid in
  the rest of the system.

## Severity rationale

- Function-contract violation: name says "to_max_bytes", behavior
  doesn't enforce that.
- Reachable when an operator configures \`max_segment_bytes\` smaller
  than the largest UTF-8 char (4 bytes) AND the segment starts with
  multi-byte content. Realistic only for misconfiguration or hostile
  input designed to exercise edge cases — but the bug exists.
- The inline comment at line 1419-1422 justifies "return the last full
  character rather than returning an empty string" — that's a
  *behavior choice*, but it violates the function's declared contract.
  Either the contract should change (function name + docs) or the
  behavior should respect the cap.

## Fix sketch

Two reasonable directions:

(A) **Strict contract** (preferred — matches function name):
  In the fallback branch, if the last character's byte length exceeds
  max_bytes, return \`String::new()\` rather than returning the
  oversize char. Callers that want "best-effort" can opt in via a
  separate API.

(B) **Document the exception**:
  Rename to \`trim_utf8_tail_with_min_one_char\` or update the doc to
  state that the result MAY exceed max_bytes when max_bytes <
  smallest character. Also update \`enforce_segment_size_for_persistence\`
  to assert / handle the over-cap case explicitly.

## Regression tests

Three tests pin the contract:
- \`trim_utf8_tail_returns_at_most_max_bytes_for_multibyte_char\` —
  \`trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes("中", 1)\` returns a string ≤ 1 byte.
- \`trim_utf8_tail_handles_2_byte_cap_with_3_byte_char\` — same for
  \`("a中", 2)\`.
- \`enforce_segment_size_respects_kept_bytes_le_max_bytes\` — the wrapper
  never produces a segment whose content exceeds max_segment_bytes.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/ingest.rs\` only. Existing tests at
lines 6051-6068 don't cover this case (max_bytes=4 with "café" tests
only the well-fitting path).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:00:25.710169Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:57.324478Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T18:03:57.324241Z","close_reason":"Fixed in commit 4668d1bf: trim_utf8_tail_to_max_bytes now respects max_bytes as a hard ceiling. Removed the 'fall back to last full character' branch that returned 3-byte 中 when max_bytes=1 or 2. New behavior: when no suffix fits within max_bytes, return empty — downstream enforce_segment_size_for_persistence already wraps the oversize signal in a Gap segment with a truncation_reason, so an empty-content gap is the sound default. 3 regression tests pin: (1) trim('中', 1) and trim('中', 2) return ≤max_bytes; (2) trim('a中', 2) returns ≤2 bytes; (3) enforce_segment_size_for_persistence on '中' with max_segment_bytes=1 produces kept_bytes ≤ 1. All 164 ingest tests pass against /tmp/ft-rv-p0-target including the original 3 trim_utf8_tail tests (no regression on the in-bounds path).","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-fbee5","title":"pattern pack discovery followed symlinks out of tree","description":"# [HIGH] pattern-pack loader: symlink-escape via discover_packs_from_dir

**Subsystem:** patterns / scan_pipeline
**File:** crates/frankenterm-core/src/patterns.rs

## The hole

`load_pack_from_file(path, root)` routes through `sandbox_resolve` only when a
`Some(root)` is supplied. `discover_packs_from_dir`, used for
`.ft/patterns/**` discovery from a workspace root, was calling
`load_pack_from_file(path, None)` for every file + rules.toml it iterated —
so the sandbox check was **skipped** even though the dir itself was
workspace-attacker-controlled.

A hostile repo could ship:

    .ft/patterns/config.yaml -> /Users/victim/.ssh/id_rsa

`path.is_file()` follows the symlink (returns true), `load_pack_from_file`
opens the target, feeds its contents to `serde_yaml::from_str`, and on parse
failure emits:

    tracing::warn!("Skipping invalid user pack {}: {e}", path.display());

Which can echo excerpts of the target file via serde's error reporting
(`mapping expected, found: …`).

## Pre-conditions

* victim clones a hostile repo that contains a symlink under
  `.ft/patterns/`;
* frankenterm loads user packs at session start.

## Fix

`discover_packs_from_dir` now passes `Some(dir)` as the sandbox root to
`load_pack_from_file`. `sandbox_resolve` canonicalizes and rejects any
resolved path outside `dir`. Added regression test
`discover_packs_rejects_symlink_escape`.

## Residual risk

A top-level symlink replacing `.ft/patterns` itself would still escape
because the caller (`load_packs_from_config`) builds `ws_dir =
root_path.join(".ft").join("patterns")` without canonicalizing. That's a
follow-up — tracking separately.

## Sibling work

- 33e84436 security(patterns): add sandbox_resolve helper (helper introduction)
- e8397747 / 8e57c8e0 fix(limits): MAX_PACK_BYTES 16 MiB cap (orthogonal DoS layer)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T17:26:10.019601Z","created_by":"p3-review-audit","updated_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.244142Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T17:54:22.243799Z","close_reason":"Fixed in 6ab52765 — canonicalize + in-tree check applied to pack discovery","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ft-05hfm","patterns","security"]}

 {"id":"ft-fs42k","title":"connector_outbound_bridge: dedup uses event.timestamp_ms (parallel ft-dijpe miss)","description":"## Defect

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1027\` passes
\`event.timestamp_ms\` (caller-supplied via the public
\`OutboundEvent::with_timestamp_ms\` builder, line 284) to
\`check_and_record\` as the dedup eviction clock — the exact shape that
ft-dijpe just fixed in the inbound bridge.

The ft-dijpe defect description even claimed *"Outbound bridge doesn't
have this shape — it uses its own \`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now()\`
for all internal state."* That claim is wrong. The outbound bridge's
constructor (line 245) does use \`SystemTime::now()\` to seed
\`timestamp_ms\` by default, but:

1. The public \`with_timestamp_ms\` builder (line 284) lets any caller
   override that default to an arbitrary value.
2. \`route_event\` line 1027 then passes that caller-controlled value
   directly to \`OutboundDeduplicator::check_and_record\`, which calls
   \`evict_expired(now_ms)\` and pops anything whose
   \`now_ms - inserted_at_ms > ttl_ms\`.
3. Same replay-bypass attack as ft-dijpe: a caller submitting two
   copies of the same correlation_id with the second copy's
   \`timestamp_ms = ttl_ms + 1\` triggers eviction of the first entry
   and the replay is treated as new.

## Concrete failure path

1. Internal caller (workflow handler, retry loop, test harness) builds
   an \`OutboundEvent\` and calls \`with_timestamp_ms\` to override the
   default \`SystemTime::now()\` (legitimate reason: replaying historical
   events for backfill, fixture-driven tests, or a buggy clock-advance
   helper).
2. First dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=0\`.
3. Second dispatch with \`correlation_id=X, timestamp_ms=ttl_ms+1\`.
4. \`evict_expired\` sees \`now_ms=ttl+1 > inserted_at=0+ttl\` → pops X.
5. \`check_and_record\` finds cache empty, inserts a new X, returns
   \`true\` → second dispatch routes as if new. All downstream
   side-effects fire twice (slack post, JIRA ticket, GitHub PR
   comment, credential broker call, etc.).

Trust model is weaker than inbound's — outbound events are
internally constructed rather than attacker-supplied — but the
defense-in-depth argument applies. A buggy internal caller that
mis-sets \`timestamp_ms\` should not silently bypass dedup, and
symmetry with the inbound bridge's hardening makes the codebase
easier to reason about.

## Severity rationale

- Same shape as ft-dijpe (silent dedup bypass), one severity level
  lower because the trust model is internal-only.
- Reachable today via any internal caller misusing
  \`with_timestamp_ms\`. Future caller bugs are likelier than
  attacker reach.

## Fix sketch

Mirror the ft-dijpe fix: in \`route_event\` line 1027, compute
\`now_ms\` from \`SystemTime::now().duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH).as_millis()\`
and pass that to \`check_and_record\` instead of \`event.timestamp_ms\`.
The \`event.timestamp_ms\` field stays useful as an analytics signal
(end-to-end source→sink latency, dispatch-history correlation) but
must NOT determine when dedup entries expire.

## Regression test

\`bridge_dedup_ignores_event_timestamp_ms_for_eviction\`: dispatch X
with \`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=0\`, then dispatch X with
\`correlation_id='r', timestamp_ms=u64::MAX/2\`. Assert the second is
\`deduplicated=true\`. Pre-fix the second returns \`deduplicated=false\`
because evict_expired with the spoofed future-timestamp pops the live
entry.

## Scope

\`crates/frankenterm-core/src/connector_outbound_bridge.rs\` only.
The line-1080 \`let now_ms = event.timestamp_ms\` (used downstream for
\`created_at_ms\`, \`broker_checked_at_ms\`, \`dispatched_at_ms\`) is a
separate concern — those telemetry timestamps arguably should also
be SystemTime::now() but that's a wider change. This bead covers
ONLY the dedup eviction clock.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"pane0","created_at":"2026-04-22T17:25:28.629996Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.140282Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:26:19.139925Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of ft-ptjlp (same defect, same line, parallel filing race). Sibling agent already applied the SystemTime::now() fix in working tree at connector_outbound_bridge.rs:1037-1044 with their own ft-ptjlp tag in the inline comment. Closing this bead so ft-ptjlp owns the fix and the test work. Reviewed their fix — it's identical to what I would have written (SystemTime::now() → dedup_now_ms → check_and_record), correctly preserves event.timestamp_ms for downstream telemetry uses.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"ft-z01cl","title":"[reality-gap] ft robot checkpoint/context/work/fleet/profile families return robot.not_implemented","description":"# Claim (README)

README.md:37-38 markets "**Robot Mode API**: JSON/TOON interface
optimized for AI agents to control other AI agents" as a core value
prop — "the closest analogy is Kubernetes for terminal-based AI
agents". This is not a footnote; it's in the opening paragraph.

# Reality (code)

Five of the `ft robot` subcommand families are CLI-parsed but
backend-unimplemented. At `crates/frankenterm/src/main.rs`:

    :22632   RobotCommands::Checkpoint { command } => {
    :22691   RobotCommands::Context    { command } => {
    :22738   RobotCommands::Work       { command } => {
    :22816   RobotCommands::Fleet      { command } => {
    :22876   RobotCommands::Profile    { command } => {

Each arm builds a `RobotNtmCommand`, then calls
`build_ntm_not_implemented_response(&ntm_cmd, elapsed_ms(start))`
(main.rs:10121) which emits a `robot.not_implemented` envelope with:

    "Command `ft robot <family> <action>` is recognized but not yet
     implemented. This command will be available in a future release.
     NTM equivalent: <command>."

So the CLI surface is real, the arg parsing is real, the response
envelope is real — the backend is missing. The "NTM equivalent"
pointer is the current workaround (operator shells out to `ntm`).

# Scope

Subcommands covered by this gap (15+ concrete actions):

- Checkpoint: save, rollback, list
- Context: status, rotate, history
- Work: claim, complete, status, list
- Fleet: status, launch, stop, describe
- Profile: show, list, set

Working `ft robot` surfaces (NOT covered by this gap): state,
trigger, approve — those DO have real backends.

# Gap severity

- README treats Robot Mode as a pillar feature.
- Users following the opening paragraph to `ft robot fleet launch ...`
  get a `robot.not_implemented` envelope at first invocation.
- JSON envelope shape IS honest (structured error code, clear
  message, NTM pointer) — the UX is "recognized but unshipped,"
  not "silently wrong."
- The honest error makes this a DOCS gap more than a correctness
  bug. Priority is for a README disclosure, not an urgent code fix.

# Recommended disposition

Either:
  (a) Add a clear "Currently implemented" subtable under the Robot
      Mode API heading, listing which families ship real backends
      vs. return `robot.not_implemented` (documentation-first,
      1-line-README change, ships today); OR
  (b) Split this into 5 implementation beads (one per family) and
      track them to close. `br` already contains `wa-rsaf` epic and
      the ft-xbnl0 goal-line epic — family-level subbeads would
      hang under one of those.

(a) is the immediate unblocker; (b) is the real closure path.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T20:44:10.888900Z","created_by":"reality-check","updated_at":"2026-04-23T00:18:56.189657Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T20:46:37.162999Z","close_reason":"Landed docs fix (disposition (a)) in 0e1432fc.  {"id":"ft-z1yhs","title":"session_restore::load_checkpoint_by_id silently accepts mux_pane_state row count ≠ stored pane_count","description":"# Defect

`crates/frankenterm-core/src/session_restore.rs:473-481` returns `CheckpointData { pane_count, pane_states }` where:

* `pane_count` is the value stored in `session_checkpoints.pane_count` at save time (stored claim).
* `pane_states` is the rows actually loaded from `mux_pane_state WHERE checkpoint_id = ?1` (actual rows).

Nothing in the load path asserts these match. Line 1204-1205 logs both counts but does not fail on disagreement. Post-ft-ybtyg, the state_hash at `session_checkpoints.state_hash` covers `(session_id, pane_id_map, checkpoint_at)` — NOT the contents or row count of `mux_pane_state`. So:

* Adding a bogus `mux_pane_state` row (checkpoint_id matches) → `pane_states.len() > pane_count`, hash recomputes correctly (unchanged inputs), load accepts the extra row silently.
* Removing a legitimate `mux_pane_state` row → `pane_states.len() < pane_count`, same as above.

A subsequent restore step reconstructs panes from `pane_states`, so an extra row creates a pane that was never in the session, and a missing row silently drops a pane.

# Threat model

DB-write is already fatal, so this is hardening against:
1. Naive filesystem-level tampering (e.g., hostile repo ships a crafted `ft.db` for operator to open).
2. Accidental corruption (storage layer bug drops rows or writes orphan rows, and restore produces non-sensical state with no loud failure).
3. Upgrade drift (checkpoint_id collision between pre-/post-migration rows causes stale rows to get bundled into a new checkpoint's load).

# Fix

In `load_checkpoint_by_id`, after loading `pane_states`, assert `pane_states.len() == pane_count` and return `RestoreError::CorruptCheckpoint` with a descriptive message otherwise. Cost: one integer comparison per load.

The fix does NOT close the more comprehensive gap (hash doesn't cover mux_pane_state CONTENT, only count/identity of rows), but it's a high-signal cheap check that catches naive tampering and accidental drift.

# Regression tests

Two symmetric tests:

* `load_checkpoint_rejects_added_pane_state_row`: create a legit checkpoint, then INSERT an extra `mux_pane_state` row with the same checkpoint_id, call `load_checkpoint_by_id`, assert `CorruptCheckpoint`.

* `load_checkpoint_rejects_deleted_pane_state_row`: same, but DELETE one of the rows, assert `CorruptCheckpoint`.

# Severity

MEDIUM — no crash, no data loss on the happy path. Silent wrong-answer on the tampered/corrupt path. Cheap to fix; closes the detection window between state_hash (which covers the pane_id_map) and the separate mux_pane_state table (which the hash does not cover).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:12:38.027641Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T17:18:06.693662Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T17:18:06.693343Z","close_reason":"Withdrawn: the count-mismatch assertion would false-positive in the real write flow where finalize_restore writes session_checkpoints.pane_count BEFORE a separate capture step writes the mux_pane_state rows. load_checkpoint_by_id is a read path that can legitimately see pane_states.len()=0 while pane_count>0 in that window. Re-examine at a higher layer (is the hash scope wrong? is there a two-phase atomicity guarantee?) rather than the naive row-count check I drafted.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
